text: Hello, this is Roxanne Liu from Oakland, California, with a video on managing a large gap between the posterior rectus sheath during a robotic ETEP of a primary ventral hernia.
The robotic instruments are docked on the left side, crossing over to cut approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba through the posterior rectus sheath.

The surgeon enters the peritoneal cavity, encountering difficulty in developing a preperitoneal space due to the extremely thin peritoneum, which tears upon attempting to cut the posterior rectus sheath.
So this is a setup for a giant gap between the posterior rectus sheath and can be quite tricky to manage.
The surgeon encounters a primary ventral hernia with a defect approximately five centimeters wide, featuring a large hernia sac resembling the size of a grapefruit.
The surgeon has completed the division of the ipsilateral posterior rectus sheath and is now operating intra-abdominally and pre-peritoneally, navigating within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon attempts to dissect the ipsilateral edge of the peritoneum free from the surrounding tissue to access the area outside the hernia sac. The defect is visible just beyond the edge of the sac.
The surgeon attempts to dissect outside the hernia sac into an avascular plane, revealing the well-defined sac characteristic of primary ventral hernias.
The surgeon uses a combination of gentle pushing and cautery to locate an avascular plane around the hernia sac, occasionally encountering extra fat lobules during the dissection.
The surgeon uses scissors like a hot blade to shave through multiple layers of the hernia sac, searching for a plane where the sac can be easily pulled out.
The surgeon presses to separate the hernia sac from the subcutaneous tissue or dermis, revealing a distinct avascular plane.

The surgeon navigates within the avascular plane, attempting to recruit enough of the hernia sac to patch the posterior gap. The sac appears large, and the surgeon assesses the size of the gap to determine the necessary amount of sac to use.

The surgeon completes the takedown of the hernia sac, opting to remove the entire sac due to the ease of dissection. During this process, the anterior rectus sheath of the contralateral side is pulled into the dissection plane and divided.
The surgeon carefully identifies the edge between the hernia sac and the anterior fascia, ensuring the sac remains attached to the peritoneum on the contralateral side to maintain its pedicle.

Sometimes I do have trouble doing this and I have to essentially completely disconnect the sac and that's fine because you're just you're using it as a biologic patch essentially because you know better than any mesh you can open up from the shelf.
The surgeon continues the preperitoneal dissection below the umbilicus, preparing to open the posterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon opens the posterior rectus sheath on the contralateral side near the arcuate line, maintaining a parallel orientation to the linea alba approximately one centimeter away.

The surgeon performs preperitoneal dissection, noting the lateral orientation of the rectus muscle. The tissue becomes thin, leading to entry into the peritoneal cavity on the ipsilateral side, while the contralateral side appears similar.
The surgeon carefully creates space to open the posterior rectus sheath without damaging the linea alba. The thin layer is visible, providing a barrier to exclude the mesh from the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon has opened a significant amount of preperitoneal space, exposing the posterior rectus sheath. There is visible bleeding from the ceiling of the surgical field, which the surgeon addresses to prevent hematoma formation.
The surgeon peels back the peritoneum and continues to open the posterior rectus sheath, maintaining a safe distance from the linea alba to avoid damaging the anterior rectus sheath.
The surgeon continues to develop the contralateral retrorectus space, preparing to evaluate the posterior defect.

The surgeon displays a large sac that has been taken down, revealing a significant hole in the posterior rectus sheath. The sac is positioned to cover the hole effectively, preventing bowel from entering the retrorectus space.
The surgeon completes sewing to patch up the entire gap in the posterior rectus sheath, using the sac to cover the defect and exclude the bowel from the retroactive space.
